headline: The Skeeters Are Built Different, the Skidmarks Blew a Layup, and Someone Needs to Call the Wonderdogs a Grief Counselor
subheadline: Week 3 of BeerLeague Baseball had a historic ERA, the most frustrating tie of the season, and proof that pitching luck is just the universe trolling you personally.
body:
## Thriller of the Week

### Buttville Skidmarks vs. General Hospital — 4-4 Tie

A tie in fantasy baseball is basically the sports equivalent of a handshake deal that leaves everyone angry. The **Buttville Skidmarks** brought a 2.25 ERA to a knife fight and somehow walked away without the win. Meanwhile, **General Hospital** countered with 30 RBIs and 70 strikeouts from their rotation — which sounds great until you realize their pitching staff was cooking at a 5.48 ERA, which is less "ace" and more "guy who gives up three runs in the third and then shrugs." Nine categories, perfectly split, zero satisfaction. This is the kind of result that haunts a manager in Week 10 when they're one game out of a playoff spot. Both teams needed this win badly, and both teams are now entering Week 4 with the energy of someone who just watched the series finale of *Lost* — confused, vaguely angry, and unsure what to believe in anymore.

---

## The Week's Defining Moment

Look, we're three weeks in and the **Sugar Land Skeeters** are doing something genuinely uncomfortable to watch if you're anyone else in this league. Their 8–2 demolition of the **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** wasn't a fluke — it was a masterclass. Matt Olson went full *Moneyball* protagonist mode (3 HR, 8 RBI, .375 OBP), Drake Baldwin posted a .406 OBP like he was auditioning for a Billy Beane spreadsheet, and their pitching staff — just casually, no big deal — threw 64 innings at a 2.11 ERA. That's the lowest ERA by any team in a winning effort this season, barely edging out the 2.10 General Hospital posted in Week 2. (By the way, a 2.11 ERA over 64 innings in a weekly fantasy matchup is the statistical equivalent of showing up to a beer league softball game with a radar gun. Rude. Extremely rude.)

The Skeeters won eight of ten categories and have now done this for three straight weeks. Three. Straight. Weeks. At some point we have to stop calling it a hot start and start calling it a résumé. This roster is constructed, not assembled. Their pitching depth is absurd, their lineup has multiple OBP weapons, and they have the kind of category balance that wins titles. The Twins, for their part, weren't embarrassing — they threw 76.2 innings and whiffed 75 batters. They just ran into a buzzsaw. Welcome to the 2024 Skeeters Experience.

Meanwhile, the other story from Week 3: **Hiroshima Radiation** woke up. After two weeks of underperforming relative to their paper roster, they went out and smashed the **Canberra Wombats** 8–2, posting 6 Quality Starts, 75 strikeouts, and a 3.10 ERA. Bryce Harper's .552 OBP is genuinely unfair, and their offense went 64-for-204 with 33 runs scored. The Wombats' own Gunnar Henderson hit 5 homers and drove in 8 runs, which in a normal week would've been enough to carry a team — but the Radiation just had too many weapons firing at once. Consider this the "we're back" announcement that every Hiroshima manager has been waiting to send.

---

## Matchup Deep-Dives

### Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs def. Albuquerque Bombers, 5-4

The **Wonderdogs** won this one despite posting a .306 OBP and being outhit 53-to-42. They survived on the backs of their pitching staff — 67 innings, 4.30 ERA, 1.13 WHIP, 5 QS, and 7 NSVH — which was miles better than the **Bombers'** bloated 5.05 ERA. The Bombers hit 9 home runs and scored 30 runs, which should feel better than a 5-4 loss. Acuña, Turang, and Stewart all had productive weeks, but when your pitching is bleeding runs, the offense has to be historically dominant to compensate. It wasn't. Both teams feel unresolved heading into Week 4.

### Sugar Land Skeeters def. Abbotsford-Colby Twins, 8-2

*(See above. Just... see above. I don't want to pile on.)*

The **Twins** threw 76.2 innings and it didn't matter. Their offense outside of Nimmo and Alvarez is running on fumes. If those two go cold, this team has a problem. Week 4 is a must-produce situation.

### Athletic Park Dragons def. Wisconsin Rapids Reapers, 6-3

James Wood (3 HR, 8 RBI, .636 OBP — *point-six-three-six*, let that breathe) and Willson Contreras (3 HR, 11 RBI, .594 OBP) turned the **Athletic Park Dragons** into an offensive freight train. They scored 40 runs and posted a .412 OBP as a team. The **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** countered with a 1.65 ERA and a 1.04 WHIP — which is a historically dominant pitching performance — but only scored 34 runs and managed a .326 OBP. Losing with a 1.65 ERA is like losing a race in a Ferrari because you forgot to put gas in it. The Reapers are top-five in pitching talent but their offense needs an intervention. Stat of note: Reapers posted 10 NSVH. Ten. And still lost.

### BaseballPro77 def. North Correa, 5-3

Jordan Walker launched 5 home runs this week. That's it. That's the sentence. **BaseballPro77** has been quietly consistent across three weeks — never flashy, always there, like the reliable second act of a prestige drama. **North Correa** stole 11 bags (more on this in a second) and Chris Sale threw 22 Ks, but a 4.73 ERA over only 51.1 innings was their undoing. You can't win pitching categories with that few innings pitched.

### Riverside Roughnecks def. Benchwarmers, 6-3

The **Riverside Roughnecks** had zero Quality Starts and a 5.04 ERA — and won 6-3. Trevor Story drove in 9 runs. That's the whole story. Zero QS, 5.04 ERA, Trevor Story. The **Benchwarmers** only threw 42.1 innings from their rotation, which is what happens when your starters keep getting pulled in the third inning. This result was fluky, the Roughnecks know it, and the Benchwarmers are licking wounds they maybe deserved.

### Hiroshima Radiation def. Canberra Wombats, 8-2

*(See above again. Hiroshima looked great, Wombats looked cooked despite Henderson going nuclear. Moving on.)*

---

## Lucky/Unlucky Team of the Week

**Lucky:** **Benchwarmers.** I want to be clear about what happened here. The Benchwarmers posted a 5.53 ERA, threw only 42.1 innings — a league-worst — and still walked away with a 6-3 win. Their hitters carried the entire roster like it was a *Weekend at Bernie's* situation and the pitching staff was Bernie. They had the highest luck delta in the league this week (+0.91). This is unsustainable in the way that eating Taco Bell every day is unsustainable — fun for a while, catastrophic eventually.

**Unlucky:** **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs.** Let me explain why this hurts to type. The Wonderdogs outpaced the Bombers in strikeouts (62 vs. 39), ERA (4.30 vs. 5.05), WHIP (1.13 vs. 1.39), QS (5 vs. 3), AND saves/holds (7 vs. 1) — and still nearly lost. They carry the season's worst luck delta (-0.60). Their pitching is legitimately good and keeps getting rewarded with a participation trophy and a frown emoji. At some point the baseball gods have to balance the books.

---

## League Record Watch

North Correa's 11 stolen bases in Week 3 ties the current season record for most steals by a team in a single week — and it's a record they set themselves. Oneil Cruz alone had 6 steals, which is the kind of number that makes you feel personally inadequate as a person who walks places. The problem: they still lost 5-3. You can win the stolen base category by 20 and still get punched in the mouth by ERA and innings pitched. North Correa is basically the *Fast & Furious* franchise — absurdly fast, but the plot keeps getting them in trouble.

---

## Manager Spotlight: Buttville Skidmarks

Here is the most interesting bad situation in the league right now. The **Skidmarks** are sitting on a 2.25 ERA — that's an elite number, that's a top-tier pitching performance — and they're not in the top half of the power rankings. Why? Because a 2.25 ERA with only 48 innings pitched and a single Quality Start is like having a Ferrari engine in a go-kart. The horsepower is there; the frame isn't holding it together. Elly De La Cruz is electric, the ERA is real, but the pitching volume issue is going to keep haunting them.

They're at No. 10 in the power rankings after a tie against General Hospital that, on paper, they should have won outright. The Skidmarks have roster talent — nobody's disputing that — but they need more innings from their starters or they'll keep winning ERA while losing the categories that require you to actually be *on the mound*. Week 4 is critical. If they can get their rotation to give them 60-plus innings and maintain that ERA, they're a playoff contender. If not, they're going to be the team that finishes with the best ERA and a .500 record, which is the fantasy baseball equivalent of leading the league in FIP but finishing fourth in your division. The talent is real. The deployment needs work.

---

## Trade Value: Buy, Sell, or Hold?

**Matt Olson (Sugar Land Skeeters):** Three home runs, 8 RBI, and a .375 OBP in Week 3 as part of the league's best squad. If anyone's holding Olson on a team outside the top four, they're either delusional about their situation or quietly loving the production. **HOLD** — but on a contending team, he's worth a premium ask in any trade conversation.

**Gunnar Henderson (Canberra Wombats):** Five homers, 8 RBIs, a legitimately dominant week — on a team that got crushed 8-2. Henderson is doing everything right; his team is doing everything wrong. This is the "sell high before the frustration turns into complacency" moment. **SELL** — or at minimum, see what you can get. When a stud is producing on a sinking ship, someone always overpays for the talent.

**Oneil Cruz (North Correa):** Six stolen bases in a single week. Six. He's a walking stolen base machine and the stolen base category is genuinely winnable every single week with him in your lineup. **BUY** — if you're a team that needs speed, Cruz is your guy. The power is real too. He's being undervalued in most trade conversations right now.

---

## Power Rankings

1. **Sugar Land Skeeters** | Three weeks of category dominance, elite pitching ERA of 2.11 this week, and an 8–2 beatdown make them the undisputed No. 1 | ➡ same
2. **Athletic Park Dragons** | James Wood (3 HR, 8 RBI, .636 OBP) and Willson Contreras (3 HR, 11 RBI, .594 OBP) powered a 6–3 win; offense is elite | ⬆ up
3. **BaseballPro77** | Jordan Walker's massive 5-HR week carried a 5–3 win; consistent category producer across three weeks | ➡ same
4. **Hiroshima Radiation** | Bounced back emphatically with an 8–2 rout, 6 QS, and 75 Ks; Bryce Harper's .552 OBP is a weapon | ⬆ up
5. **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** | Lost 6–3 but posted a freakish 1.65 ERA and 1.04 WHIP; pitching is elite, offense needs to wake up | ⬇ down
6. **General Hospital** | Tied despite Manny Machado (3 HR, 11 RBI) and a 70-K pitching week; 5.48 ERA is a concern but talent is undeniable | ➡ same
7. **North Correa** | 11 SB week is elite and Chris Sale delivered 22 Ks, but 4.73 ERA and only 51.1 IP hurt badly in the loss | ➡ same
8. **Riverside Roughnecks** | Won 6–3 on the back of Trevor Story's 9 RBI despite zero QS and a 5.04 ERA; fluky but effective | ⬆ up
9. **Albuquerque Bombers** | Acuña, Turang, and Stewart all had strong weeks, but the pitching (5.05 ERA) cost them a winnable matchup | ➡ same
10. **Buttville Skidmarks** | Elly De La Cruz is electric and the ERA (2.25) is elite, but a tie against General Hospital with this pitching staff is a missed opportunity | ⬇ down
11. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** | Crushed 8–2 despite decent pitching (3.99 ERA, 75 Ks); bats outside Nimmo and Alvarez are underperforming | ⬇ down
12. **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** | Shota Imanaga (2.70 ERA, 14 Ks) and a 4.30 team ERA should be winning more; the luck just isn't coming | ➡ same
13. **Canberra Wombats** | Gunnar Henderson (5 HR, 8 RBI) is a stud but the team fell 8–2; pitching gave up too many categories | ⬇ down
14. **Benchwarmers** | Won this week but carried by hitters alone; 42.1 IP and a 5.53 ERA is unsustainable and the luck will run out | ➡ same

---

## Waiver Wire

Nothing notable to report this week on the wire — which honestly tracks, because everyone in this league is apparently locked in and not making moves. Either rosters are set, or managers are in denial. Both are valid Week 3 strategies. (One of them is wrong.) Check back next week when the panic adds start rolling in like they always do around Week 4 when people realize their closer situation is a dumpster fire.
